title: Chip design from the bottom up – Reiner Pope
summary: Reiner Pope delivers a technical lecture on chip design fundamentals, starting from logic gates and progressing to GPU, TPU, and FPGA architectures. The talk covers systolic arrays, data movement costs, caching strategies, and comparative analysis of CPU, GPU, and brain computational structures.
writeup: Reiner Pope, CEO of MatX, walks through the silicon-level mechanics of AI chip computation from first principles: starting with AND gates and working up through partial products, full adders (3-to-2 compressors), and the Dadda multiplier to show exactly how a multiply-accumulate unit is built. The through-line is that the multiply-accumulate operation is not an arbitrary convention but the natural primitive because every step of a matrix multiply is precisely one multiply-accumulate, and the algebra collapses cleanly to p x q AND gates plus p x q full adders for a p-bit by q-bit operand pair. The talk closes on the practical design tradeoff of FP4 versus FP8 silicon allocation, establishing that these precision formats are not fungible in hardware and represent a hard architectural choice.
key_points: The fundamental primitive inside an AI chip is multiply-accumulate: one multiply of two low-precision operands followed by addition into a higher-precision accumulator, which maps directly to the innermost step of a matrix multiply (output[i,k] += input[i,j] x input[j,k]).

AI chips intentionally use asymmetric precision: low-precision multiplication (e.g., 4-bit) feeds into a higher-precision accumulator (e.g., 8-bit) because rounding errors compound across the many accumulation steps of a matrix multiply, not across the single multiplication step.

A p-bit by q-bit multiply-accumulate requires exactly p x q AND gates to generate partial products and exactly p x q full adders (3-to-2 compressors) to reduce those partial products to a final sum, via the Dadda multiplier approach.

A full adder (3-to-2 compressor) takes three single-bit inputs in the same bit position and produces a 2-bit output encoding 0 through 3; this is the largest logic primitive typically used, and it captures what humans do naturally when summing a column of binary digits.

FP4 and FP8 multiply-accumulate units are not fungible on chip: the choice of how much area to allocate to each precision format is a hard design decision, typically balanced by equalizing the power budget between the two formats, not by treating them as the same configurable resource.

chapters.title: Introduction: Building Up From Logic Gates
Multiply-Accumulate as the Core AI Primitive
Long Multiplication by Hand: AND Gates and Partial Products
Full Adder: The Largest Logic Gate
FP4 vs FP8 Fungibility and Chip Design Tradeoffs
